issue_title: Remove ALIAS operator List / Dict
issue_body:
Previously we had syntax:

```
module: exec.R
  a: 1
  b: 2
  @ALIAS: input = list(param1=a, param2=b)
```

where `input` is a script variable for `exec.R` not `a` or `b`. And we have similar stuff for Python. But this (and more!) can also be achieved by:

```
module: R(input = list(param1 = a, param2 = b)) + exec.R
  a: 1
  b: 2
```

The only difference is that the 2nd implementation will introduce additional script variables `a` and `b` but this does not harm. @stephens999 we will remove ALIAS operator. But on a second thought I'm going to put it in "Unsupported Features" page, because I imaging it is neater when:

```
module: exec.R
   a: 1
   b: 2
   c: 3
   d: 4
   (and many more parameters here)
   @ALIAS: input = list()
```

that is, `list()` means to shove all parameters to `input`. Again, not a feature we will use often or advertise, but occasionally we might find a need.

issue_title: Proposal ref(publisher): change the publication loop logic
issue_body:
Right now the publisher uses a ttl to expire the key of the application if the container and the open are available. This works just fine until you start hundreds of container because at that point the 10 seconds loops never ends.

Why not change the logic to publish the port when the container starts and use the loop to check if the container is and the port opened and remove it if not available?
```
if s.IsPublishableApp(containerName) && !s.IsPortOpen(hostAndPort) {
  s.removeEtcd(keyPath, false)
}
```

With this change the interaction with etcd is just 1 request per application when is not available instead of  `O(n)` every 10 seconds (to update the ttl) (n is the number of apps running in the local node)

issue_title: Rewrite components in Go so they are resilient, testable, and self-publishing
issue_body:
There's been some discussion and debate in #2701 about how to approach refactoring components. The core maintainers had an architectural meeting today and decided to move forward with a Go-based rewrite of the components, with the following goals:
- ~~No more Docker-in-Docker for the builder component -- instead, bind-mount the host's Docker volume so we can use that Docker graph~~ WONTFIX: This introduces security issues (breakouts in container builds can affect the host OS)
- Better log output for components, showing the component's actual status and helpful details when it fails (no more `waiting for confd to write initial templates` as in #2906)
- Components are more easily testable and less fragile (such as builder - see #3578)
- confd runs in-memory, enabling us to vendor some of its code so we can use native templating logic
- Shared Go-based libraries (with unit tests!) for code common among boot scripts
- Boot logic in a component is minimal thanks to the previous point ^
- Cleaner code and improved control flow made possible by use of Go idioms and channels (for example, rewriting the SSL certificate generation logic)

A long-term goal here is to at some point in the future be able to say `docker run -P deis/builder:2.0` and have the component come up and publish itself with everything it needs self-contained.

**Implementation details/questions**
- Do we use go-extpoints or not? An argument against is that it lends itself well to plugins, but that may not be as useful in this implementation

**Bonus**
It would be great to address the following as part of this refactor:
- nginx template rewrite (#3536)
- better handling of Ceph OSD creation errors (#2519)

replaces #2701

issue_title: Supported Python versions
Pip command not found with locally installed pip
issue_body:
If you look at `_get_pip_path` you can see it does not handle pip installs on machines that are:
1. Not windows
2. Not in a virtualenv
3. Pip is not installed at `/usr/bin/pip`

https://github.com/cloudify-cosmo/wagon/blob/master/wagon.py#L448-L449

This happened for me because pip is installed here (default location by install from get-pip.py):
```
gnicholas$ which pip

Which results in the following when I try to create a package:

gnicholas$ wagon create . -r requirements.txt 
Creating archive for ....

issue_title: conda install --channels documentation is outdated
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...**
  - [x] bug report
  - [ ] feature request


## Current Behavior
<!-- What actually happens?
     If you want to include console output, please use "Steps to Reproduce" below. -->
The `conda install --channels` [argument documentation](https://conda.io/docs/commands/conda-install.html) includes a reference to a non-existent feature, the special channel `system`, which is confusing to new users and was brought up as an issue on the [Google Group](https://groups.google.com/a/continuum.io/forum/#!topic/conda/FrwQabL-yCg). This feature no longer exists, and is deprecated in any code paths that I checked, the current use of `system` looks to be limited to options which apply to the root environment, or referring to the overall machine itself.

## Expected Behavior
<!-- What do you think should happen? -->

The documentation should accurately reflect correct values for the `--channel` argument.

## Resolution

I've submitted [a PR for this issue](https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/7163), but feel free to reject it and provide other text if it is insufficient as submitted.

issue_title: pip-installed numpy wheel not importable within conda environment
issue_body:
## Numpy failing in conda's root environment

1. Starting with Jessie Lite as a base image on a Raspberry Pi, I install miniconda3 and then I proceed to install pip with `conda install pip`. This is all done on the `root` environment of conda.

2. Next, I install the numpy wheel with the following command:
```
pip install numpy --extra-index-url https://www.piwheels.hostedpi.com/simple
```

3. Then, I try importing the numpy module inside the python (v3.4.3) interpreter and I get the following error:
```

Python 3.4.3 |Continuum Analytics, Inc.| (default, Aug 21 2015, 00:53:08)
Type 'copyright', 'credits' or 'license' for more information
IPython 6.2.1 -- An enhanced Interactive Python. Type '?' for help.

In [1]: import numpy
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ImportError                               Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-1-d4cdadb62aa7> in <module>()
----> 1 import numpy

/opt/miniconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/numpy/__init__.py in <module>()
    164     from . import fft
    165     from . import polynomial
--> 166     from . import random
    167     from . import ctypeslib
    168     from . import ma

/opt/miniconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/numpy/random/__init__.py in <module>()
     97 with warnings.catch_warnings():
     98     warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", message="numpy.ndarray size changed")
---> 99     from .mtrand import *
    100
    101 # Some aliases:

ImportError: /opt/miniconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/numpy/random/mtrand.cpython-34m.so: undefined symbol: PyFPE_jbuf
```
I've done this multiple of times, so I'm fairly sure this is repeatable.

## Numpy imported within system-wide space

Next, I did something else, and that was to reflash the sd card with a fresh Jessie Lite again (we wouldn't want to have the environment contaminated at all) and install numpy system-wide with conda not installed.
Basically, I did the following things:
```
$ sudo apt-get install python3 python3-pip
$ sudo pip install numpy --extra-index-url https://www.piwheels.hostedpi.com/simple
```
Afterward, I launched the python interpreter and imported the numpy module, and it worked.

## Bottom Line

Bottom line is that on a Raspberry Pi, while in conda's environment, a pip-installed numpy module can't be imported whilst in the system-wide environment it works fine. The same wheel has been used for installing in both scenarios.

Now, you might ask yourself why haven't I used the `conda install numpy` command?
Well, first of all, keep in mind I'm developing a system for installing packages in offline mode (non interactive mode).

The issue is a lot of python packages can't be installed from continuum's repositories on a Raspberry Pi, because they are yet to be available. Because of this, I need to fill the void by installing packages with pip, but doing this creates another 2 problems:
1. Because I'm installing packages with pip and conda, there are moments when I get the infamous `Cannot remove entries from nonexistent file ***/site-pack
ages/easy-install.pth`  error, which can by the way, be avoided by adding the `--ignore-installed` option. Even with that, it makes me nervous thinking that errors like these can appear. And when updates are being done without a human's supervision, things can get ugly - so I can't trust that everything will go smoothly all the time.
2. A mechanism for installing/updating packages with conda and pip together is going to be inherently more complex than having just one tool. And since I'm only interested in installing python packages, pip seems to be the best choice, because there are a large number of packages. The only problem with the packages on PyPi is that for many of them, I don't get the wheels, but instead, the source code, which needs to be build. As an alternative to that, there is https://github.com/bennuttall/piwheels, which has pre-compiled wheels.

So as you can see, due to the lack of conda packages for python for the `armv7` architecture, I'm going to be stuck with pip and piwheels. I'm still going to use conda as an environment manager. To me, the conda + pip combo looks better than virtualenv + pip.

## TL;DR

Pip-installed numpy package can't be imported in conda's environment while being on a Raspberry Pi. Is there anyone else who's encountering this issue? Does anyone know what the problem is?

https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/common/platform.py

issue_title: Suggestion: Add an option to reset environments
issue_body:
I think it may be a nice idea to add a conda command which resets an environment as a shortcut to deleting it and creating a new environment with the same name.

Why would someone need that? Well, I have testing environments which I use to test new packages or reproducing issues. After testing I usually want to clear all installed packages. Some command like `conda reset -n testenv` or  `conda clear -n testenv` would be very convenient.

issue_title: Misleading error when trying to create environment from missing file
issue_body:
## Current Behavior ### Steps to Reproduce When running the following command with a non-existant environment file: ``` $ conda env create -f nonexistant.yml SpecNotFound: Can't process without a name ``` This doesn't make sense. The environment file can contain the name of the environment to be created, so the `-n` option is not required in this case. ## Expected Behavior Instead, I should get an error for a missing file, maybe something like the following: ``` $ conda env create -f nonexistant.yml Given environment file path 'nonexistant.yml' does not exist ``` ## Environment Information

issue_title: conda breaks after using conda
issue_body: This has happened twice now. I get a fresh install of conda. Things like conda list work, then I use a command from the cloud site to get a netcdf4 package, **conda install -c conda-forge netcdf4=1.2.5**, and right after it is done, conda is no longer recognized as a command. Conda list, conda install, nothing works any more. It seems as if the activate.bat file just disappears (I looked and its not there) after I run that install command, so then I can no longer use an anaconda prompt.

issue_title: How to avoid superceding?
issue_body:
I was trying to install a Conda package during a demo.  I was surprised to see that the Conda package plan decided to use a Conda package I created end of 2015 and that is available in my channel.  I understand why matching packages from explicitly listed channels might  have preference, even if it results in a downgrade.  But is there a way to say "don't do that?" I imagine if I had a "newer" version in an explicitly listed channel then I might expect different behavior than an older version, but maybe not.

And in the case of Conda (unsurprisingly) there are clearly dangers to having your own conda package in a channel.  It isn't a dependency of the listed `genepattern` package, so this is just the *"automatically update conda and conda-env"* behavior that is then pulling in my custom Conda build.  Which then totally breaks my Anaconda distribution (`conda` is now gone from my installation -- I will have to hand-patch it back in, I think)

issue_title: Signatures are not secure or safe
issue_body:
Taking a look at the signature verification in conda, I see several issues which result in the signature system being fairly broken as is.
- Currently it's using 1024 bit RSA keys (according to [teoliphant](https://twitter.com/teoliphant/status/614173906358943744)). This is not a safe size for RSA keys, there is some indication that some nation state adversaries can already brute force a private key from a public key, and attacks only get better and faster. This should be at least 2048 if not 4096.
- It's using "Textbook" or "Raw" RSA. This is really bad and more or less breaks the security of the crypto system. The exact math behind it is opaque to me, but you can see it being discussed [here](http://rdist.root.org/2009/10/06/why-rsa-encryption-padding-is-critical/), [here](https://crypto.stackexchange.com/questions/20085/which-attacks-are-possible-against-raw-textbook-rsa), and many more papers and presentations from cryptographers about the topic if you google for it.

My suggestion would be to deprecate this feature immediately and try to attract a cryptographer or a security engineer to help design the secure system. In particular the folks behind [TUF](http://theupdateframework.com/) have been extremely helpful and will likely be the solution used by pip/PyPI. At the very least you should just use gpg or NaCl for signatures if you don't want to or can't find someone willing to help.

issue_title: create an option to disable menu installation in Windows OS
issue_body:
related to #1717 and #2136
Please create an option or flag to disable menu installation upon "conda create" in the Windows OS. As mentioned in the related issues, this will give an option to circumvent the PermissionError problems in Windows when creating a new conda environment in an admin installation as a non-admin on Windows.

rationale (may be TL;DR):
People that care about this, usually have a use case where they want to be flexible installing packages as non-admins (because for example going via the admin has too much lead time) but still want to offer a system-wide miniconda system with the environments centralized to users that don't know or care about how to create environments but want these environments maintained system-wide.

issue_title: ActiveHDL 10.2 not detected
issue_body:
It appears in Active HDL 9.3, there is `vsimsa.bat` and `VSimSA.exe`, but in 10.2 it changed to `vsimsa.bat` and `vsimsa.exe`.

When running VUnit against ActiveHDL 10.2 it does not detect the simulator. It looks for VSimSA (case sensitive), which it does not find.

As a possible fix, I changed the search to use `vsimsa`, which allows ActiveHDL to be detected, but this then causes confusion between RivieraPRO and ActiveHDL since they use the same method of detection (e.g. even if VUNIT_SIMULATOR=rivierapro then ActiveHDL will be used if first on the path).

One difference between activehdl and riviera is that activehdl bin directory has `avhdl.exe` and riviera has `riviera.exe` (this is on windows -- not sure how it looks on Linux). However, even if the two simulators can be successfully detected and differentiated, then an absolute path will be needed to invoke `vcom` and `vsim`, because otherwise the order in the system path will take precedent.

Related questions:

- Does VUnit want to support Active HDL 9.x? (user guide only claims support for 10.2). 9.x is getting pretty old now. I personally wouldn't mind not having it supported.
- How can the detection / differentiation between Active HDL and RivieraPRO be improved?
- Should VUnit provide a way to explicitly specify a path to simulator to use in cases where multiple versions of the same simulator are on the path?

issue_title: Verilog parser won't detect an instance within generate
issue_body:
The following is not detected correctly by `verilog_parser.py`:

```
module name;
genvar i;
  generate
    for( i=0; i < 10; i = i + 1 ) 
      begin: INST_GEN
        true1 instance_name1();
    end
  endgenerate
endmodule
```
It takes `INST_GEN` instead of `true1`.

A unit test to demonstrate this can be found [here](https://github.com/feddischson/vunit/commit/533818b6c37df97a1aa59f3519cf93602b8e046f ). I further added a pull-request for this unit-test.

issue_title: 'allocation' returns failure when already optimized
issue_body:
I have a cron job that runs {delete, allocation, optimize} nightly and house it with a script that intercepts STDOUT and curator's return code in order to determine success of the run. I have some expectation of idempotence in these operations and so I use the same selection parameters each time.

In the case where an operation would be applied twice to the same criteria (e.g. right after), I notice that 'delete' will WARN (+msgout?) and return zero ("No indices matched provided args"), where 'allocation' will WARN and return one:

```
Running command: "/usr/local/mycorp/prod/local/python_2.6/bin/curator --host 'elasticsearch' --loglevel 'WARNING' --port '9200' --timeout '21600' allocation --rule 'tier=warm' indices --newer-than '30' --older-than '1' --time-unit 'days' --timestring '%Y.%m.%d'"
WARNING   Very large list of indices.  Breaking it up into smaller chunks.
WARNING   No indices to act on.
WARNING   No indices to act on.
WARNING   No indices to act on.
WARNING   Job did not complete successfully.
```

I understand that I should be using the API for my case and ought not to expect the CLI verbs to map to API functions directly, but should these be unified somehow? Is there a decision on what constitutes success?

issue_title: Monthly reindex works but claims it failed
issue_body:
After automatically creating a monthly index with date math, I reindex all the daily indexes in to it using date math and a wild card. This works but is only "successful" (success exit code) if I've statically named the destination index. 

As you can see from this partial index list, all 3 reindex runs worked correctly. But only the "*-bar-*" one actually reported success.

>yellow open   filebeat-bar-monthly wSwjAfaKRounkqh1IvrcAg   5   1     503571            0    105.7mb        105.7mb
>yellow open   filebeat-foo-monthly TseDd9_oQEaqFTxIcAmY0Q   5   1     503563            0    105.5mb        105.5mb
>yellow open   filebeat-2017.07-monthly _7ndFTFdRnGeV52wFErsmA   5   1     503668            0    105.6mb        105.6mb

(ignore the minor difference in doc count, this test cluster had data going in to one of the daily indices)

The request bodies for each are as follows. There seems to be a problem with the date math indicator even when no date math is performed..

Works and "succeeds"
> 
      request_body:
          source:
            index: '<filebeat-{now/M{YYYY.MM}}.*>'
          dest:
            index: 'filebeat-foo-monthly'

Works but "fails"
> 
      request_body:
          source:
            index: '<filebeat-{now/M{YYYY.MM}}.*>'
          dest:
            index: '<filebeat-bar-monthly>'

Works but "fails"
>
       request_body:
          source:
            index: '<filebeat-{now/M{YYYY.MM}}.*>'
          dest:
            index: '<filebeat-{now/M{YYYY.MM}}-monthly>'

The error messages are in the format

2017-07-22 16:20:52,421 ERROR     curator.actions.reindex  _post_run_quick_check:1266 **Index "<filebeat-{now/M{YYYY.MM}}-monthly>" not found after reindex operation**. Check Elasticsearch logs for more information.

issue_title: RequestsHttpConnection::perform_request except clause uses possibly-unbound 'response' variable
issue_body:
When using helpers.bulk (which is fantastic!), I received:
    UnboundLocalError: local variable 'response' referenced before assignment

This was raised by connection/http_requests.py RequestsHttpConnection::perform_request(); see: https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/master/elasticsearch/connection/http_requests.py#L70

The (small) error is that the `try:` block in that function attempts to set `response`, but this assignment could fail if an exception is thrown from requests.Session::request(); in this case, `response` is unbound in the `except:` clause, but is unconditionally dereferenced. 

An example of one possible patch; not fully tested:
https://github.com/brianfaull/elasticsearch-py/commit/89f51899a671ebd195eff22f20f624fa8783139f

Overview: Split the assembly of the request from the sending of the request, and use the request object (not the response object) for logging. Clearly there are other possibilities also.

This is not an issue for the analog within http_urllib3.py (however it seems that url and full_url/path_url arguments to self.log_request_fail() are transposed between http_urllib3.py and http_requests.py unless I misunderstand.)

I note your preference for signing the CLA and submitting a PR; please let me know if that is still your preference for a fix as small as this.

Many thanks for a wonderful and useful library!

issue_title: Method to wait for healthy cluster, that can be called before ES connection exists
issue_body:
This code:
```
  es = Elasticsearch(["elasticsearch:9200"])
  es.cluster.health(wait_for_status='yellow')
```
Fails with:
```
index_test_data_1  | Traceback (most recent call last):
index_test_data_1  |   File "indexer.py", line 74, in <module>
index_test_data_1  |     main()
index_test_data_1  |   File "indexer.py", line 55, in main
index_test_data_1  |     es = init_elasticsearch()
index_test_data_1  |   File "indexer.py", line 34, in init_elasticsearch
index_test_data_1  |     es.cluster.health(wait_for_status='yellow')
index_test_data_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/elasticsearch/client/utils.py", line 76, in _wrapped
index_test_data_1  |     return func(*args, params=params, **kwargs)
index_test_data_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/elasticsearch/client/cluster.py", line 33, in health
index_test_data_1  |     'health', index), params=params)
index_test_data_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/elasticsearch/transport.py", line 314, in perform_request
index_test_data_1  |     status, headers_response, data = connection.perform_request(method, url, params, body, headers=headers, ignore=ignore, timeout=timeout)
index_test_data_1  |   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/elasticsearch/connection/http_urllib3.py", line 175, in perform_request
index_test_data_1  |     raise ConnectionError('N/A', str(e), e)
index_test_data_1  | elasticsearch.exceptions.ConnectionError: ConnectionError(<urllib3.connection.HTTPConnection object at 0x7fa33e09f110>: Failed to establish a new connection: [Errno 111] Connection refused) caused by: NewConnectionError(<urllib3.connection.HTTPConnection object at 0x7fa33e09f110>: Failed to establish a new connection: [Errno 111] Connection refused)
```
I use docker-compose to start `index_test_data` (Python script) and elasticsearch at the same time. The `ConnectionError` is because nothing is listening on port 9200 yet.

I would like a method like this:
```
es.wait_for_status('yellow')
es = Elasticsearch(["elasticsearch:9200"])
```
That will swallow the `ConnectionError`'s, and block until elasticsearch is up.

issue_title: azure-cli version 2.0.31 breaks "azure login --msi"
issue_body:
---

### Environment summary

I am installing azure-cli in a docker image, based on Centos 7, using yum.

issue_title: [Testing] Test framework should support running in parallel
issue_body: The VCR tests currently run in series. This used to be necessary because multiple tests could reuse resource groups or other resources. These issues have been resolved and it would be beneficial if the tests executed in parallel, particularly during recording where certain long running operations can take several minutes to execute. This will be crucial when entire test suites need to be re-recorded.

issue_title: Doc of how to adjust op status polling interval
issue_body: Is there a document and example showing how to control the interval at which Azure Python SDK polls operations for completion? For example, creation of managed disk should take only a few seconds, but when waiting for the Azure Python SDK to complete it appears to take 30 seconds (likely due to polling interval being ~30 seconds). It would be great to be able to adjust the poll interval to decrease the overall run-time of the operation.

issue_title: `azure-client` module unavailable via pip
issue_body:
It appears that the `azure-client` module is unavailable:
```

Collecting azure-client
  Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement azure-client (from versions: )
No matching distribution found for azure-client
```

This error is occurring with `pip 9.0.1 (python 3.5)`. Other packages (`azure-common`, `azure-mgmt`) install correctly.

See [Other Azure libraries for Python](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/python/api/overview/azure/other?view=azure-python) for reference.

issue_title: How to download publicly available objects from blob storage?
issue_body:
I have some files in blob storage that I want publicly available, but I"m unsure of how someone would fetch them.

It looks like the [`azure.storage.common.storageclient module`](https://azure-storage.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ref/azure.storage.common.storageclient.html) is the right way to go, but It's unclear how to use this.

issue_title: SAS Key in Python SDK differs from C# by a leading '?'
issue_body:
`var sasToken = share.GetSharedAccessSignature(sharedAccessFilePolicy);` in c# will provide a sas token but with a '?' at the front.

The python equivalent, `sas_token = fs.generate_share_shared_access_signature(FILE_SHARE_NAME, expiry=expiry, permission=file.models.SharePermissions(read=True, write=True, delete=True, list=True))` does not.

The issue lies in that if you try to use the C# key, the python sdk fails. Simply trimming the leading '?' allows it to work.

issue_title: Performing distance to point queries
issue_body:
One of the queries we're using bullet for is distance from point to `btCollisionObjects`.  There is a very real possibility that what we are doing is the wrong way around and would be considered an abuse of Bullet.  That said, we've discovered an error.  I'll provide a high level discussion of the error here and have code that reproduces the problem attached.

Test Scenario
==========
Create a shape (either sphere, box, or convex hull of the box).  It floats above the origin such that the distance from the origin to the nearest point on the shape is 0.25 units.  We then take a sequence of test points in the range [0.24, 0.25] (in this case, 101 samples so that the test point starts at a distance of 0.01 and moves closer by 0.0001 at each sample.)

At each query, we look at the known distance, a derived distance (the distance between the query point and the *nearest* point on the shape to that point), the reported normal from the shape to the query point.

When running the example on the command line, you can specify the test shape as a command line parameter, e.g.,
  - `./a.out sphere`
  - `./a.out box`
  - `./a.out convexhull`

The problem
==========

There are actually several problems:

1) For the box and convex hull, as the point gets closer and closer to the surface (in a monotonically decreasing manner), the reported normal direction flips directions arbitrarily.
2) It should be possible to compute the closest with high precision in these simple scenarios.  However, for the convex hull, we are getting an error in distance on the order of 1e-10-1e-12, and for the box, 1e-9-1e-10.  This last is particularly surprising.
3) When querying against the sphere shape, the point on the sphere given as closest is *always* the sphere center, regardless of the radius.

Example Code
===========
[bullet_point_query.zip](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/files/658483/bullet_point_query.zip)

issue_title: Energy Conservation Error - Restitution Example
issue_body:
First of all: I'm really enjoying pybullet! Thank you.

I am currently investigating different physic engines for simulation purposes. As a starting point I've basically taken the restitution.py example and played a little bit with it. 

I came across an interesting effect: For small heights of the ball, the ideal bouncyness works fine and the energy / momentum is conserved. If the initial height is set to 10 m, the ball will lose energy when the simulation advances. This does not vary with the step size, solver iterations or substeps.

The resting height of the ideal bouncing ball is ~4.5 m. 

I found some older post in the forum regarding this issue:

-[Energy-conserving collisions?](https://pybullet.org/Bullet/phpBB3/viewtopic.php?p=&f=&t=4703)
-[Restitution dependend on timestep](https://pybullet.org/Bullet/phpBB3/viewtopic.php?t=11860)
-[How can I increase the physical realism of my simulation](https://pybullet.org/Bullet/phpBB3/viewtopic.php?p=&f=9&t=10695)

Is there any explanation / progress with regards to this topic?
I am using double precision, varied step size, switched on splitImpulse, varied erp values. 

An example of the code is attached.  ( Matplotlib is used in a hacky way to observe the system ).
![bouncing_ball_energy_loss](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20998300/35432427-4f28119c-0280-11e8-955e-a485d8975c93.png)

Code:

```python
#you can set the restitution (bouncyness) of an object in the URDF file
#or using changeDynamics

import pybullet as p
import pybullet_data as pbd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.animation as animation
import time

def T(v):
	return 0.5 * 10. * v**2
def U(x):
	return 10. * 9.81 * x-1.

cid = p.connect(p.SHARED_MEMORY)
if (cid<0):
	cid = p.connect(p.DIRECT)

# Add a data path to load plane
p.setAdditionalSearchPath(pbd.getDataPath())

# Load the plane
plane = p.loadURDF("plane.urdf")
p.changeDynamics(plane,-1,restitution=1.)

# Load the ball
sphere = p.loadURDF("../../robots/ball-e/urdf/ball-e.urdf",[0,0,10])
p.changeDynamics(sphere,-1,restitution=1.)


p.setRealTimeSimulation(1)
p.setGravity(0,0,-9.81)
p.setPhysicsEngineParameter()



# Set up the measurements
x = np.zeros((2,500), dtype=float, order='C')
w = np.zeros((2,500))

# Make a simple plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(3, 1, sharex=True, figsize = (10,10))


while True:
	p.stepSimulation()

	# Get measurements
	# Get the position
	pos,orn = p.getBasePositionAndOrientation(sphere)
	# Get velocity
	vel, rot = p.getBaseVelocity(sphere)

	# Manipulate the array
	x[:,:-1] = x[:, 1:]
	# Write new entries
	x[0,-1] = pos[-1]
	x[1,-1] = vel[-1]

	# Manipulate the array
	w[:,:-1] = w[:, 1:]
	# Write new entries
	w[0,-1] = U(pos[-1])
	w[1,-1] = T(vel[-1])

	# plot
	ax[0].clear(), ax[1].clear(), ax[2].clear()
	ax[0].plot(x[0]), ax[0].plot(np.mean(x[0])*np.ones_like(x[0]))
	ax[1].plot(x[1]), ax[1].plot(np.mean(x[1])*np.ones_like(x[0]))
	ax[2].plot(w[0]+w[1]), ax[2].plot(np.mean(w[0]+w[1])*np.ones_like(x[0]))
	plt.pause(0.001) 
```

issue_title: Connections from Pool Keeps Getting Old Data from Database
issue_body:
I have an API that executes code similar to this:
```
mysql = await create_pool(host='HOST', port=PORT, user='USER', password='PWORD', db='DB', loop=asyncio.get_event_loop())
async with mysql.acquire() as conn:
        async with conn.cursor() as cur:
            query_string = "SELECT * FROM table;"
            await cur.execute(query_string)
            rows = await cur.fetchall()
```

This works well to get the required data from the db the first time. However, on making the same request, over and over again even after changing the data in the database, I noticed that the results obtained from the piece of code is different from what is in the DB. What was returned was actually the result from before making changes to the db outside of the API.

Python version = 3.5.1
MySQL version = 5.6
Environment = Fedora 24

One thing I noticed is that when I explicitly called `conn.close()` after executing the query, it worked correctly. 

I am assuming this shouldn't be how to use `conn.close()` in this case?

issue_title: [CLI] --generate-config option doesn't work
issue_body: When using the master branch with python 3.4 in ubuntu 14.04, `jupyter notebook --generate-config` doesn't seems to do anything except running the notebook.

issue_title: APIHandler.write_error should set `reason` field in JSON error response.
issue_body:
When the `@json_errors` decorator was removed, to be replaced with `APIHandler.write_error` (see #2853), the establishment of the `reason` field was not preserved.   Since `@json_errors` set that field and some notebook clients depend on its presence, it should be set in the compatible replacement.

Please see Issue #2942 for the _meandering details_ of how this conclusion came about.

issue_title: Double clicking cell enters edit mode
issue_body:
If I double click a cell output, I enter edit mode, with focus in the cell input. This only works the first time. Subsequent double clicks just select the word in the output.

Is this the intended behavior?

I always try to select a word in the output with double clicks. It seems kind of silly to enter edit mode with a double click when you can just reach above a bit and single click.

issue_title: JS script less-watch is installed with setup.py install
issue_body:
[less-watch](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/5.0.0/scripts/less-watch) is installed to bindir (such as `/usr/bin`) with `setup.py install`. According to @takluyver, it is [not desired](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/commit/75835e4ca8e45e71f49724abe41f07e2e6218401#commitcomment-21638624).

The reason for the installation is [a glob in `setup.py`](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/5.0.0/setup.py#L79) that takes all files from the `scripts` dir. Please either move that file somewhere else or change the glob to only include `pjoin('scripts', 'jupyter-*')`. Thanks

issue_title: problems starting notebook; doesn't see existing files and will not allow creation of new ones
issue_body:
Out of nowhere today, when I start a new kernel / server
> jupyter notebook
a window opens up in the browser as usual, but none of the files are visible and I can't create a new one either.

I shut down my computer and updated anaconda and juypyter and tried again, but the problem persists.
I don't understand at all! I am using jupyter notebook version 4.4.1

issue_title: Escape does not close keyboard shortcut editor
issue_body:
I see in the editor that it identifies <kbd>Esc</kbd> as mapping to "close pager". I'm not sure what does and doesn't count as a pager, but I would have thought that it would close the modal dialog.

@carreau @gnestor happy to fix, just point me in the right direction.

issue_title: Improving 4.x nbextensions
issue_body:
@fperez @damianavila @bollwyvl @sccolbert teoliphant @minrk @takluyver @ijstokes

I have been having a lot of on- and off-line discussion this week about the current state of nbextensions in 4.x. For a long time, we (at least this was my own logic) have hesitated to make any significant changes to how 4.x nbextensions are installed/loaded/packaged, because we know that much bigger changes are on the way in 5.0. After these recent conversations, I am convinced that we need to improve the existing 4.x nbextension architecture in the meantime. The current situation in 4.x is causing way to many problems for users and devs.

This goal of this issue is to 1) raise our community awareness that we need to do something about this and 2) come up with a concrete proposal for moving forward.

## Current pain points of 4.x nbextensions

nbextensions can be installed in the following locations:

```python
In [2]: paths.jupyter_path('nbextensions')
Out[2]: 
['/Users/bgranger/Library/Jupyter/nbextensions',
 '/Users/bgranger/anaconda/envs/python34/share/jupyter/nbextensions',
 '/usr/local/share/jupyter/nbextensions',
 '/usr/share/jupyter/nbextensions']
```

Config can be loaded from the following locations:

```python
In [4]: paths.jupyter_config_path()
Out[4]: 
['/Users/bgranger/.jupyter',
 '/Users/bgranger/anaconda/envs/python34/etc/jupyter',
 '/usr/local/etc/jupyter',
 '/etc/jupyter']
```

By default, installed extensions are not loaded, until activated. The **only** place extensions can be activated is in the *users* config directory (`~/.jupyter/nbconfig/notebook|notebook.json`):

```python
In [13]: cat /Users/bgranger/.jupyter/nbconfig/notebook.json
{
  "load_extensions": {
    "widgets/notebook/js/extension": true,
    "create_assignment/main": true,
    "nbgrader/create_assignment": true
  }
}
```

**Pain Point #1**: even though nbextensions and config can be installed in *system*, *sys.prefix* or *user* paths, the list of extensions to activate is only loaded from the *user* config. Thus, there is no way of activating nbextensions at the system or `sys.prefix` level.

* Example 1a: Because of this a JupyterHub deployment can't enable the various nbgrader extensions at the system level - each user has to do it. 
* Example 1b: Continuum can't enable extensions on a conda env basis, even though they can install them there.

**Point Point #2**: There is no standard package format for nbextensions (other than a directory of stuff) and no standard way of copying an nbextension into place. Because of this, there are multiple, separate hacky ways of packaging and installing nbextensions.

* Example 2a: nbgrader has created custom subcommands, such as `nbgrader extension install` and `nbgrader extensions activate`.
* Example 2b: Continuum is starting a couple of new projects to try to solve these issues in the conda context.
* Example 2c: We ourselves, just gave up and hardcoded the ipywidgets nbextensionss in the frontend code.
* Example 2d: Projects like https://github.com/takluyver/cite2c have a separate `install.py` script that installs and activate its extension.
* Example 2e: @minrk recommends just using `ls -n` to "install" his nbextensions here: https://github.com/minrk/ipython_extensions

## Proposal for addressing these pain points.

Here is the overall approach:

* Solve the above pain points in a full backwards compatible manner.
* Release the fixes quickly in a 4.2 release.
* In 5.x on the exiting pages we have (notebook/tree): formally deprecate the 4.x approach to loading/installing nbextensions, our existing JS APIs and CSS classes.
* In 5.x on the new JupyterLab page: only use new APIs and the new npm based plugin approach.

Here are the technical details of the proposal:

**Pain Point 1**

@takluyver has made an excellent point that the "nbconfig" frontend configuration system deliberately only loads from the users config (`~/.jupyter`) because these are really mean to only be "user preferences". I completely agree with this and others I have spoken to also concur. So we can't make that part of our app start to load system or `sys.prefix` based config.

I propose to start to load nbextension activation config from *all* config paths (system, `sys.prefix` and user) *always*, but do that instead by throwing that data into the `page.html` template itself, rather than loading later using the nbconfig web service. This would allow us to keep system data out of the user level app-preferences but still load config for nbextensions from all locations. This is not difficult to implement and is fully backwards compatible.

**Pain Point 2**

This one is more difficult and my proposal will be more controversial. Today, in many cases, people are shipping JS code in Python packages. For 5.0 we are going to stop doing that and embrace npm as our package format and manager, but that would require breaking changes in 4.x, so is not on the table.

I propose, that for 4.2+ we embrace putting nbextensions into Python packages:

* Don't standardize specifically where in the python package they are. This allows existing packages such as nbgrader to not have to change anything.
* Instead, 1) provide metadata in `setup.py` that gives the package relative paths of those assets and 2) provide that same metadata in `__init__.py` to enable runtime inspection. Something like this:

```python
>>> import nbgrader
>>> nbgrader._jupyter_nbextension_paths
['nbextensions/assignment_list', 'nbextensions/create_assigment']
```

* Using this simple convention, we could then improve the existing `jupyter nbextension` command line tool to work with python packages: `jupyter nbextension install nbgrader` and `jupyter nbextension enable nbgrader`. We could also include flags the target installation/config to the *system*, *sys.prefix* and *user* directories. This would again be fully backwards compatible.
* Using this simple convension, Continuum can build conda installers for nbextensions with almost no effort.

issue_title: Text output processing needs to handle backspace
issue_body:
Originally reported as takluyver/bash_kernel#50, but it affects other kernels - e.g. run `!man ls` in an IPython notebook.

Certain programs (like `man`) appear to use an overwriting system for simple formatting, so `A<BS>A` makes a bold A and `_<BS>A` makes an underlined A. Where programs don't understand this, the second character replaces the first and you see unformatted text. However, we don't handle backspaces at all, so we show `AA` and `_A`.

This should go along with our handling of ANSI escapes and carriage returns.

issue_title: Is there any similar mechanism like arg_scope
issue_body:
Dear Deepminders:

I just wonder is there a similar mechanism existing in the current sonnet repo resembling arg_scope?

Usually I would like to put all declarations of sub-modules in the initializer, but sometimes it looks a little redundant:

```
class Model(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, name = "model"):
        super(Model, self).__init__(name = name)

        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            self._batch_norm_1 = BatchNorm(decay_rate = 0.9, update_ops_collection = None)
            self._batch_norm_2 = BatchNorm(decay_rate = 0.9, update_ops_collection = None)
            self._batch_norm_3 = BatchNorm(decay_rate = 0.9, update_ops_collection = None)
            self._batch_norm_4 = BatchNorm(decay_rate = 0.9, update_ops_collection = None)
            self._batch_norm_5 = BatchNorm(decay_rate = 0.9, update_ops_collection = None)
            self._batch_norm_6 = BatchNorm(decay_rate = 0.9, update_ops_collection = None)
            ......

```

I know I could do it in a list:
```
class Model(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, name = "model"):
        super(Model, self).__init__(name = name)

        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            self._batch_norms = [BatchNorm(decay_rate = 0.9, update_ops_collection = None)
                                              for _ in range(num_batch_norm_modules)]
```

However, I still want to know if there is a way I can write as in the following:
```
class Model(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, name = "model"):
        super(Model, self).__init__(name = name)

        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            with arg_scope([BatchNorm], decay_rate = 0.9, update_ops_collection = None): 
                self._batch_norm_1 = BatchNorm()
                self._batch_norm_2 = BatchNorm()
                self._batch_norm_3 = BatchNorm()
                self._batch_norm_4 = BatchNorm()
                self._batch_norm_5 = BatchNorm()
                self._batch_norm_6 = BatchNorm()
            .    .....

```

The arg_scope and its usage is from the following links:
https://github.com/tensorflow/models/blob/master/research/inception/inception/slim/scopes.py
https://github.com/tensorflow/models/blob/master/research/inception/inception/inception_model.py

I just put here for discussion since the current sonnet still works well.

issue_title: RelatedFieldWidgetWrapper raises KeyError on Django
issue_body:
Affects: Django 1.11.0 but not >=1.11.1 (i never tested <1.10. *may* affect early 2.0.)

Problem: Trying to create or view a ProductClass in th Dashboard results in a KeyError on context['field'].

Bug description
-------------------
`RelatedFieldWidgetWrapper` in apps/dashboard/widgets.py: inherits from Widget but does not call super().\_\_init__.  
This means that some variables that `Widget.get_context()` builds are not present in `RelatedFieldWidgetWrapper.get_context()` (context['widget']).

When rendering ProductClass form in the dashboard, the template calls widget_tweaks's `render_field` with a class attribute added. When adding class attributes, widget tweaks will test the `BoundField` for truthyness.
In Django==1.11.0 , `BoundField` is only has \_\_len__ (which is used for truthyness tests) which eventually leads to accessing context['widget'] (which is not created). In later versions (after [this commit](https://github.com/django/django/commit/844ae40a7cdcb1738ca3a2e8f13bb6b8c2e22efd) ) BoundField got a `__bool__()` method so no access to subwidgets happens.

Solution 1: Change `RelatedFieldWidgetWrapper` to call super().\_\_init__. I don't quite understand it entirely and maybe there is a reason it doesn't, so i'm not submitting a PR
Solution 2: Require >Django==1.11.0

issue_title: Develop and publish a supported versions policy
issue_body:
Oscar does not currently have a formal policy that defines which versions are officially supported for maintenance and security releases, and the period for which versions will be supported.

I think we should develop a policy so that it is clear which versions will receive security and data-loss fixes.

My initial proposal for discussion is as follows:

1. With effect from Oscar 2.0 we use [semantic versioning](https://semver.org/) for releases.

2. We make Oscar 1.6 an LTS release that is linked to Django 1.11, which means we patch data loss and security issues for as long as Django 1.11 is officially supported (until April 2020).

3. We aim to release a new LTS one year before the previous LTS reaches end of life (i.e., April 2019 for the next LTS), and so on going forward. This is the same approach as Django takes.

4. Any major release issued in between these is **not** considered and LTS, and will only receive data loss and security fixes for a period of 1 year after it is released. This means that we drop support for Oscar 1.5.x in August 2018.

issue_title: debian packages install to the wrong location
issue_body:
according to https://wiki.debian.org/FilesystemHierarchyStandard the `/opt` directory is for "Pre-compiled, non ".deb" binary distribution".

the dotnet packages _are_ .deb packages, and so therefore should not go into `/opt`. Also, the `dotnet` symlink should go into `/usr/bin` _not_ `/usr/local/bin`. `/usr/local` is reserved for use by the system administrator.

issue_title: A less verbose cipher list?
issue_body:
(I am not a cryptographer!)

As of d6e554b1fd7eafccd6defba392676e5536a077f1, [the nginx SSL config specifies](https://github.com/letsencrypt/lets-encrypt-preview/blob/d6e554b1fd7eafccd6defba392676e5536a077f1/letsencrypt_nginx/options-ssl.conf) the following `ssl_ciphers` value:

> ssl_ciphers "ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256 ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384 ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-SHA ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-SHA ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-SHA256 ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-SHA384 ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256 ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384 ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA256 ECDHE-RSA-AES256-SHA384 DHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256 DHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384 DHE-RSA-AES128-SHA DHE-RSA-AES256-SHA DHE-RSA-AES128-SHA256 DHE-RSA-AES256-SHA256 EDH-RSA-DES-CBC3-SHA"

I'm wondering if this is actually necessary or if we can do it in a much less verbose way.

I've had decent luck with a configuration like this:

> ssl_ciphers DEFAULT:!aNULL:!eNULL:!ADH:!RC4:!RC2:!DES:+HIGH:+MEDIUM:!LOW:!SSLv2:!EXP:!MD5:!DSS:!PSK;

[Qualys SSL Labs' SSL test](https://www.ssllabs.com/ssltest/) gives a 90/100 rating for the cipher suite that this shorter configuration allows.

I can see another potential benefit over it simply being shorter: it allows future ciphers that aren't blacklisted to automatically work. I think a lot of the frontend web server configuration people put together comes from copy-pasting samples from various blog posts, and this `ssl_ciphers` list is basically just one long magic string.

Given that a lot of people will no doubt use Let's Encrypt as a point of reference for how to do something right, this sort of configuration would potentially give people an `ssl_ciphers` configuration that doesn't have to be revisited to determine what the latest magic string is.

Is this possible? Is there a giant problem with this approach?

issue_title: Offer a well-defined non-interactive mode.
issue_body:
For various purposes (such as scripting or our own integration tests!) people will sometimes want to run the client in non-interactive mode.  In such situations, it is a mistake to block on user input of any sort.  We should offer a clear cli flag that causes the client to stop asking the user questions.  

In non-interactive mode it may be acceptable to error out if some instruction is missing, though if at all possible that should be deterministic and happen immediately when the client is run, not partway through execution.

issue_title: AvroProducer key schema must be record
issue_body:
Looking at [Schema Registry quickstart](https://github.com/confluentinc/schema-registry) there doesn't seem to be restriction of having a record type as Kafka key:

> $ curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/vnd.schemaregistry.v1+json" \
>     --data '{"schema": "{\"type\": \"string\"}"}' \
>     http://localhost:8081/subjects/Kafka-key/versions
>   {"id":1}

Yet AvroProducer for some reason [only accepts record type](https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/blob/master/confluent_kafka/avro/__init__.py#L110).

Would it be reasonable to relax this so we can pass any valid top level avro type, or even make key serialization optional so that only the value part be required to be an avro?

issue_title: parse does not allow tzlocal in tsinfos
issue_body:
I noticed an incompatibility between verion 2.4.2, 2.5.0 and above.

I need to parse a timestamp string, that has no tz information to a datetime object with timezone.

In version 2.4.2 I was able to do:

    from dateutil.parser import parse
    from dateutil.tz import tzlocal
    ts = "01/04/17 10:00"
    d = parse(ts, tzinfos=tzlocal, dayfirst=True)

Which resulted in 

    datetime.datetime(2017, 4, 1, 10, 0, tzinfo=tzlocal())

Starting with version 2.5.0 I get an exception. I debugged into the parser module, but I can not get my head around it and do not see why it is breaking...

Obviously the __init__method of your tzlocal class has changed. In 2.4.2 the __init__ of tzlocal was inherited by ``tzinfo`` whose init looked like this:

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs): # real signature unknown
        pass

Can I consider this a bug?
Since you are allowing calling parse with a tzinfos set to a ``collections.Callable`` which has an incompatible init method?

How am I supposed to achieve the expected behaviour with version 2.5.0 and higher?

issue_title: Confusion in README for using VCAP_SERVICES
issue_body:
The way it is implemented in python-sdk, there is a confusion in the python-sdk README. It says :
```
If you run your app in Bluemix, you don't need to specify the username and password. In that case, the SDK uses the VCAP_SERVICES environment variable to load the credentials.
```
However, if somebody want to use the `VCAP_SERVICES` path then the username and passwords needs to be `YOUR SERVICE USERNAME` and `YOUR SERVICE PASSWORD` respectively in their app because it is doing string match on these to get the creds automatically. So if somebody puts something else into these variables then it is not able to take the values automatically from bluemix.

issue_title: Partial loading on nested fields
issue_body:
Given an object with a nested field, is it possible to partially load the object and all nested fields?
e.g.

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
from marshmallow import Schema, fields


class Address(Schema):
    street = fields.Str(required=True)
    city = fields.Str(required=True)
    state = fields.Str(required=True)
    zipcode = fields.Str(required=True)
    country = fields.Str(required=True)


class User(Schema):
    first_name = fields.Str(required=True)
    last_name = fields.Str(required=True)
    address = fields.Nested(Address)


payload = {
    'first_name': 'John',
    'address': {
        'zipcode': '90210'
    }
}

user_schema = User()
data, errors = user_schema.load(payload, partial=True)
if errors:
    raise Exception(errors)
```

Output:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test_partial_nested.py", line 30, in <module>
    raise Exception(errors)
Exception: {'address': {'city': ['Missing data for required field.'], 'state': ['Missing data for required field.'], 'street': ['Missing data for required field.'], 'country': ['Missing data for required field.']}}
```

Input is coming from a REST API and ideally I would like to bind all incoming payload at once.

Thanks!

issue_title: Incorrect "required" validation in marshmallow.fields.Nested
issue_body:
``` python
class UserSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.String()

class BlogSchema(Schema):
    user = fields.Nested(UserSchema, required=True)

_, errs = BlogSchema().load({})
assert 'user' in errs
assert errs['user'] == [fields.Field.default_error_messages['required']]
```

This works fine, error is reported for 'user' attribute in blog:
`{'user': [u'Missing data for required field.']}`

Although, this works differently (notice "required=True" for "name" field in UserSchema):

``` python
class UserSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.String(required=True)

class BlogSchema(Schema):
    user = fields.Nested(UserSchema, required=True)

_, errs = BlogSchema().load({})
assert 'user' in errs
assert errs['user'] == [fields.Field.default_error_messages['required']]
```

Resulting errors look like this:
`{'user': {'name': [u'Missing data for required field.']}}`
This looks even more ridiculous if your errors are even more deeply nested.

I think that error should appear only if you do this:

``` python
_, errs = BlogSchema().load({'user': {}})
```

and for previous case the correct errors should be "required" error for "user" attribute of blog.

issue_title: Support schema introspection
issue_body:
The envelope used to serialize responses is implemented in a set of helper methods (`format_items`, `wrap_response`, etc.) that could be challenging to introspect (e.g. for building swagger docs). Implementing the envelope using fields and nested schemas would be more amenable to introspection--it should Just Work with apispec.

One option would be adding some magic to `Schema`--for example, overriding `get_declared_fields` to replace the original fields with `attributes`, a `Nested` that wraps the original fields. This might be too opaque, so we could also define primary schemas separately and explicitly wrap them in `JsonSchema` classes.

Or maybe this complicates the implementation too much, and it's best to sacrifice purity and just introspect schemas manually--it shouldn't be too hard to write an apispec hook that generates the appropriate markup. The only other caveat there would be that introspection would be brittle, and likely stop working if users subclass `Schema` and change the envelopes.

Help @sloria!

issue_title: Incorrect Handling of Arrow Keys in miniterm.py
issue_body:
I stumpled upon this bug when tracing down a problem in mpfshell - a shell for micropython [1].
There the problem came up that the arrrow keys show a lagging behaviour when navigating the shell history or the command line.
Basically you press ARROW-UP, nothing happens, you press ARROW-UP again and get the arrow-up functionality once [2]. This, of course, renders the REPL of micropython pretty useless...

To find out what is received and sent by miniterm I added two lines of debugging code:

```
diff miniterm-backup.py miniterm.py
168a169
>             print("[def getkey] key list: ",list(c))
493a495
>                     print("[def writer] transformed: ",list(text), " encoded: ",list(self.tx_encoder.encode(text)))
```

This made clear that there is at least a bug in the getkey function (and probably in the encoding function)..

After the first keypress you get

```
[def getkey] key list:  ['\x1b']
[def writer] transformed:  ['\x1b']  encoded:  [27]
```

After the next single keypress you get

```
[def getkey] key list:  ['[']
[def writer] transformed:  ['[']  encoded:  [91]
[def getkey] key list:  ['A']
[def writer] transformed:  ['A']  encoded:  [65]
[def getkey] key list:  ['\x1b']
[def writer] transformed:  ['\x1b']  encoded:  [27]
```

Here getkey keeps kind of lagging behind. And I'm not sure about the correctness of the encoding...

The problem seems to be limited to Linux and Python 3.x. I've done the testing with Kubuntu 16.04-64,
Python 3.5.2 and pyserial 3.2.1.


GHPS

-------------
[1] https://github.com/wendlers/mpfshell
[2] https://github.com/wendlers/mpfshell/issues/11

issue_title: Deploy an online tool
issue_body:
This issue will capture progress on making `license-expression` and `boolean.py` into online tools.

Transpiling tools that did not quite work:

1. RapydScript  https://github.com/atsepkov/RapydScript
Lacks support for importing things (like, `import __future__`), its list of importable modules [is here][1]

2. PyPyJS https://github.com/pypyjs/pypyjs
The whole thing is complicated and I failed to make it work. Main demotivator: the website offers python `2.7.9` and there is a [github issue][2] that indicates `python 3` support is stalling.

Transpiling tools that look promising:

1. Transcrypt https://github.com/qquick/Transcrypt and transcrypt.org
This is currently the only candidate that has a distinct transpiling step (you actually see the `.js` files as a result) that could be made to work. However, there are problems which are [summarized on SO][3].

2. Brython https://github.com/brython-dev/brython
The most laid-back approach: just works, delivers results but sometimes the resulting boolean expressions come out wrong (runtime error).

Transpiling tools that look promising but not yet tried: 

1. Skulpt http://www.skulpt.org/
It looks similar to Brython, which is why untried.

2. Batavia https://github.com/pybee/batavia
Again, similar to Brython and untried.

[1]: https://github.com/atsepkov/RapydScript/tree/master/src/lib
[2]: https://github.com/pypyjs/pypyjs/issues/172
[3]: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/44463861/transpiling-boolean-py-package-to-javascript-with-transcrypt

issue_title: consider setting lowest version for numpy and scipy
issue_body:
**current**: install requires list numpy but no version number
**proposed**: use `numpy==1.10` or even earlier, like `numpy==`1.8.2` or `numpy==1.9.5`

Not everyone is using conda, and the current versions of numpy on PyPI are built with ATLAS instead of MKL and more importantly, there is no corresponding scipy for the ATLAS build on Windows because there is no _open source_ MSVC compatible version of FORTRAN :(

Therefore, when you update pvlib using `pip install -U pvlib` into your Windows system  if there is a new version of numpy on PyPI then  it will remove your MKL version and install the ATLAS version, therefore breaking your scipy. This is annoying at best and confusing and possibly very problematic at worst, especially for novice python users.

Setting the minimum version required should resolve this for most people. Perhaps a section in the wiki or docs may also be useful?

issue_title: bug in get_relative_airmass(model='youngirvine1967')
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
pvlib python implements the Young and Irvine 1967 model as:
```python
    elif 'youngirvine1967' == model:
        am = ((1.0 / np.cos(zenith_rad)) *
              (1 - 0.0012*((1.0 / np.cos(zenith_rad)) ** 2) - 1))
```

The published formula is

```
am = sec(z) * (1 - 0.0012 * (sec(z)^2 - 1))
```

See http://adsbit.harvard.edu/cgi-bin/nph-iarticle_query?bibcode=1967AJ.....72..945Y&db_key=AST&page_ind=3&plate_select=NO&data_type=GIF&type=SCREEN_GIF&classic=YES

If you squint very hard you might notice that pvlib python's implementation is equivalent to

```
am = sec(z) * (1 - 0.0012 * sec(z)^2 - 1)
```

**Versions:**
 - ``pvlib.__version__``: 0.5.2. I probably broke it when cleaning up code in the 0.1-0.2 era.

**Additional context**
Discovered when implementing #158. Write good unit tests! I'll make a pr with a fix soon.

issue_title: Add support for RequestCountPerTarget as autoscaling metric
issue_body:
[AWS added possibility to do autoscaling based on the request count per target in July 2017](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/new-target-tracking-policies-for-ec2-auto-scaling/).  The new value is called RequestCountPerTarget ([you can find it from](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/latest/userguide/as-scaling-target-tracking.html)).

Senza currently supports only [MetricTypes](http://stups.readthedocs.io/en/latest/components/senza.html) "CPU", "NetworkIn", "NetworkOut", and RequestCount could be added as a new MetricType to allow scaling by request count per target.

issue_title: Pareto Optimization
issue_body:
**Premise:** the new approach adopted by **z3** to handle *Pareto Optimization* is incremental, the user is expected to run `(check-sat)` multiple times and if any new *Pareto-front* solution exists the search returns `SAT`. When no other *Pareto-front* solution exists, wrt. those that have already been printed, **z3** returns `UNSAT`. Any additional call to `(check-sat)` after `UNSAT` restarts the *Pareto Optimization* engine, making it enumerate again all solutions.

**Issue:** if there is only one objective function on the stack, **z3** does not seem to print `UNSAT` at the second `(check-sat)` call to state that there isn't any other *Pareto-front* solution. 

On the one hand, *Pareto* search with only one objective obviously degenerates to normal optimization search. On the other hand, the solver behaviour silently changes, forcing the end-user to keep track of the number of objectives currently loaded on the formula stack in order to correctly handle the `SAT/UNSAT` result (e.g. normally an `UNSAT` would signal the end of the *Pareto-search*, but when only one objective is loaded it will not be returned so this case would need special handling).

**question:** wouldn't it be more consistent to return `UNSAT` (every other `check-sat` call) even when dealing with only one objective function, if the user has manually set the `opt.priority` setting to be equal to `pareto`? This would relieve the end-user from handling any *corner-case* when scripting/coding for problems that are highly incremental or dealing with a benchmark-set with a non-uniform (and unknown) distribution of objectives.

issue_title: Optimization seems to confuse the sign
issue_body:
This is related to https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/1339 but is different in that I think Z3 is actually producing the wrong answer here.

Consider:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_BV)
(define-fun  s1 () (_ BitVec 8) (bvneg #x14))
(declare-fun s3 () (_ BitVec 8))
(define-fun  s2 () Bool (bvsgt s3 s1))
(assert s2)
(maximize s3)
(check-sat)
(get-objectives)
(get-value (s3))
```

For this query, Z3 responds:

```
sat
(objectives
 (s3 255)
)
((s3 #xff))
```

But I'd argue that due to the call to `bvsgt` on line 5, Z3 should've figured out that `s3` is a signed quantity and hence the maximum value it can take can only be `127`.

Interestingly, a slight modification to the input where the constant s1 is not negated:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_BV)
(define-fun  s1 () (_ BitVec 8) #x14)
(declare-fun s3 () (_ BitVec 8))
(define-fun  s2 () Bool (bvsgt s3 s1))
(assert s2)
(maximize s3)
(check-sat)
(get-objectives)
(get-value (s3))
```

We now get:

```
sat
(objectives
 (s3 127)
)
((s3 #x7f))
```

which is the correct response. So, something fishy is going on indeed.

issue_title: Constructors of globally defined data types are no longer in scope after pop
issue_body:
Hi,

I want to apply Z3 for solving simple equational constraints on algebraic data terms.
I also intend to solve the generated constraints incrementally using push and pop commands.
Hence, I declare the necessary algebraic data types first. Then, I introduce a new scope for a sequence of constraints and the required declarations. Afterwards I remove this scope and repeat this procedure for the next sequence of constraints.

But it seems that by removing a scope also global information is lost, namely the constructors of the globally declared data types.
For instance, consider the following sequence of SMT-LIB commands:

~~~
; <global algebraic data type declarations>
(push)
(declare-const xs (List Bool))
(assert (not (= xs (as nil (List Bool)))))
(check-sat)
(get-value (xs))
(pop)
(push)
(declare-const xs (List Bool))
(declare-const y Bool)
(declare-const ys (List Bool))
(assert (and (= xs ((as insert (List Bool)) y ys)) (not (= y false))))
(check-sat)
~~~

Running Z3 on that script, I receive the following outputs/errors:

~~~
sat
((xs (insert false nil)))
(error "line 26 column 48: unknown function/constant insert")
sat
~~~

The same problem occurs, whenever I try to use constructor terms of polymorphic algebraic data types (which are globally declared) after the first removal of a scope via pop.
For simple (monomorphic) types like Color (see below) everything works just fine.

~~~
(declare-datatypes () ((Color red blue yellow)))
~~~

Although the information on the constructors of polymorphic types seems to be lost after leaving a scope, the corresponding types are still globally visible. Any attempt to redeclare them, results in an error.

I use Z3 version 4.4.2 running the binary directly on an SMT-LIB script with the `-smt2` option.

issue_title: ResourceNotFound exception on removetree
issue_body:
`removetree` breaks when (presumably) boto cleans up a directory after all files have been deleted.

To replicate (I am using a local minio server to test against): 

```python 
bucket_name = 'test-bucket1'

from minio import Minio
m = Minio(
    endpoint=MINIO_ENDPOINT, access_key=MINIO_KEY, 
    secret_key=MINIO_SECRET, secure=False
)
m.make_bucket(bucket_name)

from fs_s3fs import S3FS
fs = S3FS(
    bucket_name=bucket_name, aws_access_key_id=MINIO_KEY,
    aws_secret_access_key=MINIO_SECRET, endpoint_url='http://' + MINIO_ENDPOINT
)

dr = fs.makedirs('data/hello')
with dr.openbin('world', 'w') as f:
    f.write(b'somedata')

fs.removetree('data')
```

which raises

```python
ResourceNotFound                          Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-9-8b5db24ca94e> in <module>()
----> 1 fs.removetree('data')

~/anaconda3/envs/dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/fs/base.py in removetree(self, dir_path)
   1057             for _path, info in gen_info:
   1058                 if info.is_dir:
-> 1059                     self.removedir(_path)
   1060                 else:
   1061                     self.remove(_path)

~/anaconda3/envs/dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/fs_s3fs/_s3fs.py in removedir(self, path)
    636         if _path == '/':
    637             raise errors.RemoveRootError()
--> 638         info = self.getinfo(_path)
    639         if not info.is_dir:
    640             raise errors.DirectoryExpected(path)

~/anaconda3/envs/dev/lib/python3.6/site-packages/fs_s3fs/_s3fs.py in getinfo(self, path, namespaces)
    433                     obj.load()
    434         except errors.ResourceNotFound:
--> 435             raise errors.ResourceNotFound(path)
    436 
    437         if _path == '/':

ResourceNotFound: resource '/data/hello' not found
```

I assume that boto is cleaning up the directory after all files have been deleted. In which case we probably want to do something in `removedir` like

```python
try:
    info = self.getinfo(_path)
except ResourceNotFound:
    return
```

Thoughts?

issue_title: Support for flat-binary backend
issue_body:
Some users would like to read/write data to/from flat-binary files. To support this, an extension of FORMIO must be created. Before doing this, details on how data gets stored need to be flushed out. As a first pass, I propose the following specification:

1) HDF5 datasets are stored as flat-binary files
2) HDF5 groups are created as folders
3) JSON files are used to stored HDF5 attributes
4) HDF5 links are stored as soft-links
5) HDF5 references stored using some form of OID?
    - This would require more infrastructure development
6) HDF5 region references stored using same mechanism as HDF5 references (see 5)

issue_title: Form valiadion error on MoneyField
issue_body:
When I tried to create `ModelForm` based on my model containing `MoneyField`, I got error `Enter a list of values.` for the appropriate field.

After some debugging, it seems that problem is in that `djmoney.money.Money` is not instance of `list` or `tuple`, which creates a problem at [clean method of MultiValueField](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/forms/fields.py#L1009).

Simple solution is to override clean method of `MoneyField`, but it would be great if you could address this issue in `django-money` upstream.

issue_title: [play] query parameters and code persistence
issue_body:
As an initial first pass, and supported by the work in #2141, we should figure out what our URL structure should be. Here's a first pass for just repo, ref, and kernel:

```
https://play.nteract.io/?repo=nteract/vdom&ref=master&kernel=python
```

Those seem totally fine. Now onto the `code` layer...

## Persistence layer

One way to do these fiddles is to put all the code in the URL itself, URI encoded:

```
https://play.nteract.io/?code=import%20this
```

Another way would be to back these with gists (suggested by @yuvipanda, @betatim):

```
https://play.nteract.io/?gistID=5c73e81ee857686168c3abec49225482
```

Or we could rely directly on firebase (suggested by @captainsafia) and track our own storage:

```
https://play.nteract.io/?id=agQr23nuI
```

One consideration that I'd like to take into account is if/how we'd support forking from one of these. I'd like to make it immediate. As you change a previously "published" fiddle, you're running on a (virtual) fork of the original. As soon as you share/save, you've effectively committed a new version. I guess this means we have to take into consideration what a document lifecycle is with these.

Prior art (in the JS space):

* http://jsbin.com/
* https://codepen.io/pen/
* http://requirebin.com/
* https://jsfiddle.net/

/cc @yuvipanda, @mpacer, @captainsafia, @betatim

issue_body:
Hi folks,

When a `StreamField` is empty for a given translated language `wagtail-modeltranslation` doesn't fallback to the default language, as `django-modeltranslation` does for Django's standard fields.

I've traced the issue to `django-modeltranslation`'s `TranslationFieldDescriptor` [meaningful_value()](https://github.com/deschler/django-modeltranslation/blob/master/modeltranslation/fields.py#L324-L331) which, non surprisingly, doesn't account for `StreamField`'s particularities.

I'll submit a PR suggesting a patch to address this.

issue_title: install fails on arch linux because "cl" command exists, but is something different
issue_body:
I'm having trouble getting the latest pyvex to work on arch linux. I'm using a virtualenv. Install works fine with pip. When I try to import pyvex, I get the following error:

```
$ python -c 'import pyvex'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "~/.virtualenvs/pyvex/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyvex/__init__.py", line 31, in <module>
    pvc = _find_c_lib()
  File "~/.virtualenvs/pyvex/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pyvex/__init__.py", line 23, in _find_c_lib
    ffi.cdef(_ffi_str)
  File "~/.virtualenvs/pyvex/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cffi/api.py", line 105, in cdef
    self._cdef(csource, override=override, packed=packed)
  File "~/.virtualenvs/pyvex/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cffi/api.py", line 119, in _cdef
    self._parser.parse(csource, override=override, **options)
  File "~/.virtualenvs/pyvex/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cffi/cparser.py", line 299, in parse
    self._internal_parse(csource)
  File "~/.virtualenvs/pyvex/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cffi/cparser.py", line 304, in _internal_parse
    ast, macros, csource = self._parse(csource)
  File "~/.virtualenvs/pyvex/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cffi/cparser.py", line 262, in _parse
    self.convert_pycparser_error(e, csource)
  File "~/.virtualenvs/pyvex/lib/python2.7/site-packages/cffi/cparser.py", line 291, in convert_pycparser_error
    raise api.CDefError(msg)
cffi.api.CDefError: cannot parse "extern VexControl vex_control;"
:3:19: before: vex_control
```

Apparently the file `_ffi_str` variable contains only the string "extern VexControl vex_control;". 

After some digging I came to the conclusion that the issues is in [make_ffi.py#L58](https://github.com/angr/pyvex/blob/master/make_ffi.py#L58). The first tried command is `cl`. This is a binary that does something completely different (apparently part of the cliquer package) on my system.
This fails silently as there is no check for `Popen.returncode`. Maybe using `subprocess.check_output` would be better suited here, since it throws an exception on non-zero exit status?

After switching `cmd1` and `cmd2`, it properly generates the file (with `pip install -e .`). But I guess this might break other systems...

issue_title: Update examples to reflect changes to observer methods
issue_body:
As of DK 2.0.0 rc7, examples that utilize observer methods will fail because they are still using the old syntax and logic.

To fix the the issue, all examples using observer methods need the following changes:

1. call back definitions require 2 parameters, not 1
replace **def mode_callback(attribute)** with **def mode_callback(vehicle, attr_name)**

2. replace **add_attribute_observer** with **on_attribute**
3. replace **remove_attribute_observer** with **remove_attribute_listener**

Examples on python.dronekit.io need to be updated as well.

issue_title: Move the readonly settings to a capability.
Should scripts still obey "readonly"?
issue_body:
If scripts are intended to be our "escape hatch" to provide commands to administrators that shouldn't be run by users. For this reason, they may need to be run in "maintenance mode", where users' access to them is tightly constrained. For example, you may want to disable write access to users when rebuilding quotas. However, every script that makes this sort of change right now checks for whether `readonly` is set, and if it is, refuses to operate. Should they?

Argument in favor: If an administrator sets "readonly", they may expect all Kinto-related things that read the config file to be readonly. Violating that expectation may be an unpleasant surprise.

Argument against: If an administrator wants to switch user access to readonly while they do maintenance, there's no obvious way to do that. The only obvious/clean way I can think of is to run Kinto with a config file that's readonly, and then copy the file and edit it to make it not readonly for scripts.

Thoughts?

issue_title: pytraj.pca uses excessive memory
issue_body:
This is an issue that I suspect is more to do with my ineptitude than with an actual bug or flaw in the code.

I am interested in running a principle component analysis on a relatively short trajectory (hundreds of frames):

```
traj = pytraj.load('my_traj.nc','my_prmtop')
pca_data = pytraj.pca(traj,mask='!@H=',n_vecs=2)
```

Whenever I attempt this, however, all 16 GB of my machine's RAM are quickly consumed and the computation stalls. In order to debug I have attempted to perform the analysis on just one frame, and have verified that the trajectory object is tiny:

```
> traj = pytraj.io.load('different_traj.nc','different_prmtop',frame_indices=range(0, 1, 1))
> traj
pytraj.Trajectory, 1 frames: 
Size: 0.001379 (GB)
<Topology: 61680 atoms, 18601 residues, 18161 mols, PBC with box type = ortho>
```

But the problem persists. In fact, I am unable even to run this analysis on an HPC cluster with 40 GB of memory assigned (the batch system complains of too much memory usage.) Attempts with even more memory are currently pending, but surely this should not be necessary! I am using pytraj version 2.02.

---

I am aware that pytraj's pca implementation is designed to echo the implementation in cpptraj:

```
# Step one. Generate average structure.
# RMS-Fit to first frame to remove global translation/rotation.
parm myparm.parm7
trajin mytraj.nc
rms first !@H=
average crdset AVG
run
# Step two. RMS-Fit to average structure. Calculate covariance matrix.
# Save the fit coordinates.
rms ref AVG !@H=
matrix covar name MyMatrix !@H=
createcrd CRD1
run
# Step three. Diagonalize matrix.
runanalysis diagmatrix MyMatrix vecs 2 name MyEvecs
# Step four. Project saved fit coordinates along eigenvectors 1 and 2
crdaction CRD1 projection evecs MyEvecs !@H= out project.dat beg 1 end 2
```

I have attempted to use this option as well, but I get similar issues; depending on the environment in which I run cpptraj, I am either unable to start the "matrix" step due to insufficient memory, or else it begins and then segfaults. I am using cpptraj version 16.16.

---

What's the remedy here? Please let me know if there is any more information I can provide.

issue_title: Explore alternate SQL contexts
issue_body: Sparkmagic currently supports only vanilla SQLContexts as first class interfaces.  If a user wants to use an alternate context (like a HiveQLContext), they can do so through the pyspark or scala interfaces, but they must handle the context itself.  It may be useful to allow the user to specify a type of SQLContext when using the SQL interface.
